title-group.article-title: Radiofrequency for Facial & Neck Rejuvenation: A Bibliometric Analysis

fn.p: Dr Kumar is Head of Global Medical Affairs, and Ms Baek is research manager, Jeisys Medical Inc., Seoul, Republic of Korea. Dr Sethi is a consultant plastic surgeon at the Department of Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery at Fortis Hospital, Ludhiana, India. Dr Wu is a consultant plastic surgeon in private practice in Singapore.

author-notes.corresp: Corresponding Author: Dr Narendra Kumar, Global Medical Affairs, Jeisys Medical Inc., 806,96 Gamasan-ro, Geumcheon-gu, Seoul 08501, Republic of Korea. E-mail: narendrakumar@jeisys.com; Instagram: @narendrakumar226

permissions.copyright-statement: © The Author(s) 2025. Published by Oxford University Press on behalf of The Aesthetic Society.

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits non-commercial re-use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. For commercial re-use, please contact reprints@oup.com for reprints and translation rights for reprints. All other permissions can be obtained through our RightsLink service via the Permissions link on the article page on our site—for further information please contact journals.permissions@oup.com.

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: Radiofrequency (RF) devices are increasingly utilized in facial and neck rejuvenation for their ability to remodel the dermis and stimulate neocollagenesis. Although efficacy and safety have been widely reported, patient-centered outcomes remain less well synthesized. The study aims to map global research trends on RF for facial and neck rejuvenation (2015-2025), focusing on efficacy, safety, and patient-reported outcomes. A bibliometric–systematic literature review was conducted following PRISMA 2020. PubMed, Embase, Cochrane CENTRAL, and LILACS were searched for English-language studies from 2015 to 2025. Bibliometric data from Scopus were analyzed in R (Bibliometrix, Biblioshiny). Performance indicators, co-authorship, co-citation, keyword analyses, and thematic evolution were assessed. Of 1446 records, 44 studies met the inclusion criteria. Publication growth averaged 7.2% annually, peaking in 2021. The United States led output (36.4%) and citations, followed by South Korea (22.7%) and China (18.2%). International collaboration networks were limited and centered on a few prolific authors. Early studies emphasized device parameters and safety, whereas post-2020 research shifted toward clinical outcomes and patient satisfaction. Keywords highlighted wrinkles, skin laxity, and patient satisfaction as central axes. Research on RF rejuvenation has matured over the past decade, shifting from technical evaluation to patient-centered outcomes. Nevertheless, productivity is concentrated in a few regions and among a few authors, with an underrepresentation of Latin America and Africa. Broader international collaboration and consistent use of patient-reported outcome measures are needed.

body.p: Skin aging is a natural process influenced by various factors, including time, genetic predisposition, and chronic exposure to ultraviolet radiation.1 These factors disrupt the normal function and structure of the skin, leading to the development of wrinkles, enlarged pores, laxity, loss of elasticity, and pigmentation disorders.2 Such skin changes also exert a considerable negative psychological burden and negatively impact the perception of quality of life.3 As a result, there has been a growing demand for interventions aimed explicitly at facial and neck rejuvenation.4

In this context, nonablative radiofrequency (RF) devices have emerged as one of the most relevant interventions for skin rejuvenation.5 Their mechanism of action is based on the generation of heat within the dermis through the resistance of the tissue to an electromagnetic current. This process induces an immediate contraction of collagen fibers and triggers a wound-healing response that stimulates neocollagenesis independent of skin pigmentation.5

However, despite the abundant scientific literature supporting the use of RF devices, most available studies have primarily focused on describing technical parameters and assessing quantitative outcomes related to efficacy and effectiveness.5,6 As a result, qualitative outcomes, such as patient satisfaction, usability, perceived improvement, and tolerability, remain insufficiently addressed. These aspects are crucial in aesthetic procedures, because this intervention should be patient-oriented and largely determine clinical acceptance to promote adherence and use.7

In this regard, RF applied to skin rejuvenation has undergone remarkable evolution over the past decade, with the development and study of multiple modalities (monopolar, bipolar, unipolar, multipolar, fractional, and microneedling) across different technological and geographical contexts, with results reported by various authors. To understand this evolution, bibliometric analysis represents an essential tool, because it allows the identification of key authors and institutions, the mapping of knowledge networks, the evaluation of scientific productivity, and the recognition of emerging trends.8 These approaches rely on techniques for visualizing and synthesizing information, such as thematic mapping, graphical representations, network diagrams, and trend analyses.

The present study aims to analyze the scientific production on RF (fractional, ablative, microneedling, and nonablative) for facial and neck rejuvenation between 2015 and 2025, with a particular focus on outcomes related to patient experience and satisfaction with this therapy.

sec.title: Search Strategy and Data Collection
sec.p: This study adopted a bibliometric–systematic literature review approach, which integrates quantitative bibliometric analysis with the qualitative synthesis characteristic of systematic reviews.9

For the systematic review, a comprehensive search was conducted across 4 electronic databases: PubMed, Embase, Cochrane CENTRAL, and LILACS. The search covered studies published between January 1, 2015, and March 15, 2025, restricted to English-language publications. Boolean search strategies were adapted to each platform, using a combination of Medical Subject Headings (MeSH), Emtree terms, and free-text keywords related to RF and facial rejuvenation. The final search was completed on June 27, 2025, and the complete syntax is provided in Table 1.

tr.td: ((“Radiofrequency Therapy” [MeSH Terms] OR “radiofrequency” [Title/Abstract] OR “radio-frequency” [Title/Abstract] OR “radiofrequency device” [Title/Abstract] OR “fractional radiofrequency” [Title/Abstract] OR “microneedle radiofrequency” [Title/Abstract] OR “microneedling radiofrequency” [Title/Abstract] OR “non-ablative radiofrequency” [Title/Abstract] OR “bipolar radiofrequency” [Title/Abstract] OR “monopolar radiofrequency” [Title/Abstract]) AND (“Rejuvenation” [MeSH Terms] OR “facial rejuvenation” [Title/Abstract] OR “skin tightening” [Title/Abstract] OR “skin laxity” [Title/Abstract] OR “anti-aging” [Title/Abstract] OR “wrinkle reduction” [Title/Abstract] OR “skin sagging” [Title/Abstract] OR “facial contouring” [Title/Abstract] OR “neck rejuvenation” [Title/Abstract] OR “dermal remodeling” [Title/Abstract]) AND (“2015/01/01” [Date—Publication] : “2025/03/15” [Date—Publication]) AND English [Language])

(“radiofrequency” OR “radio-frequency” OR “radiofrequency device” OR “fractional radiofrequency” OR “microneedle radiofrequency” OR “microneedling radiofrequency” OR “non-ablative radiofrequency” OR “bipolar radiofrequency” OR “monopolar radiofrequency”) AND (“facial rejuvenation” OR “skin tightening” OR “skin laxity” OR “anti-aging” OR “wrinkle reduction” OR “skin sagging” OR “facial contouring” OR “neck rejuvenation” OR “dermal remodeling”) Filters applied: Year first published: **January 2015 to May 2025** Language: **English** Word variations were searched automaticallySearch conducted on **June 27, 2025**

(“radiofrequency therapy”/exp OR “radiofrequency”:ti,ab OR “radio-frequency”:ti,ab OR “radiofrequency device”:ti,ab OR “fractional radiofrequency”:ti,ab OR “microneedling radiofrequency”:ti,ab OR “microneedle radiofrequency”:ti,ab OR “non-ablative radiofrequency”:ti,ab OR “bipolar radiofrequency”:ti,ab OR “monopolar radiofrequency”:ti,ab) AND (“rejuvenation”/exp OR “facial rejuvenation”:ti,ab OR “skin tightening”:ti,ab OR “skin laxity”:ti,ab OR “anti-aging”:ti,ab OR “wrinkle reduction”:ti,ab OR “skin sagging”:ti,ab OR “facial contouring”:ti,ab OR “neck rejuvenation”:ti,ab OR “dermal remodeling”:ti,ab) AND [english]/lim AND [2015-2025]/py

((“radiofrequência” OR “radio frequency” OR “radio-frequency” OR “radiofrequency” OR “dispositivo de radiofrequência” OR “radiofrequency device”) OR (“microneedling” OR “microagulhamento”)) AND (“rejuvenescimento” OR “rejuvenation” OR “rejuvenescimento facial” OR “skin tightening” OR “flacidez” OR “anti-aging” OR “redução de rugas” OR “wrinkle reduction” OR “contorno facial” OR “facial contouring”)

sec.p: In total, 1446 references were retrieved: 362 from PubMed, 545 from Embase, 52 from Cochrane CENTRAL, and 486 from LILACS. All records were exported to Zotero Reference Manager for centralized management and initial deduplication. Subsequently, the unique references were transferred to Rayyan, where an additional deduplication step was performed, followed by the screening phase.

sec.title: Deduplication and Study Selection
sec.p: After deduplication in Zotero and Rayyan, 735 unique references were identified and screened for titles and abstracts in accordance with the PRISMA 2020 guidelines. This process was performed independently by 2 reviewers, who harmonized interpretation criteria through a pilot of 30 articles before formal screening. Discrepancies were resolved by consensus or through consultation with a third reviewer.

A total of 607 articles were excluded because they did not meet the inclusion criteria (eg, inadequate study design, non-adult populations, interventions unrelated to RF, and absence of subjective or experiential outcomes). Thus, 128 articles proceeded to full-text review, of which 44 studies ultimately met the inclusion criteria and were retained for bibliometric analysis.

sec.title: Bibliometric Analysis
sec.p: In parallel, a bibliometric analysis of the scientific literature on nonablative RF for facial and neck rejuvenation was conducted between 2015 and 2025. The final dataset was exported in CSV format and processed in R Studio (v4.3.2) using the Bibliometric package and its graphical interface, Biblioshiny.10
The following techniques were applied:

list-item.p: The bibliometric information was downloaded from Scopus, and citation counts were manually retrieved from Google Scholar and PubMed.
Scientific performance analysis: annual number of publications and citations, most productive countries, institutions, and authors.
Scientific mapping: construction of co-authorship networks (authors, institutions, and countries), reference co-citation, and keyword co-occurrence analyses.
Thematic analysis: identification of emerging topics and conceptual evolution through clustering in R Studio.

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: A total of 44 documents published between 2015 and 2025, originating from 13 different sources, were analyzed.11-54 The annual growth rate of publications was 7.18%, reflecting the increasing interest in nonablative RF for facial and neck rejuvenation. These studies involved 175 authors, with an average of 4.32 co-authors per manuscript and an international collaboration rate of 27.27%. The mean number of citations per manuscript was 13.41. The list of citations per manuscript is in Supplemental Table 1. Scientific production reached its peak in 2021, highlighting a surge in academic interest in these technologies over the last decade (Figure 1).

sec.title: Geographical Distribution
sec.p: The United States led scientific production with 16 articles (36.4%), followed by South Korea with 10 articles (22.7%) and China with 8 articles (18.2%). European countries such as France, Italy, Poland, Spain, and Germany contributed only marginally, each accounting for 2.3% of the total output (Figure 2).

The temporal analysis showed that the United States has maintained a consistent leadership in scientific production since 2015, whereas South Korea and China have experienced remarkable growth over the past 5 years (Figure 3). In terms of impact, the United States not only led in volume of publications but also in the number of accumulated citations (Figure 4).

sec.title: Authors
sec.p: The most productive authors published between 2 and 4 articles each. Productivity trends over time highlighted Goldman MP as the leading author, with the highest number of publications and consistent output throughout the study period (Figure 5). The co-authorship analysis revealed the formation of international collaboration networks, with Goldman serving as a central connector among researchers (Figure 6).

sec.title: Sources of Publication
sec.p: The most active journals were Dermatologic Surgery and the Journal of Cosmetic Dermatology, both of which concentrated a significant proportion of the included articles. Journal performance was assessed using the h-index metric (Table 2).

caption.p: List of Journals With the Number of Published Articles

tr.td: Journal of Cosmetic and Laser Therapy
Journal of Clinical and Aesthetic Dermatology

sec.title: Keyword Co-occurrence
sec.p: The keyword co-occurrence analysis identified the most frequent terms as female, adult, male, patient satisfaction, cutaneous parameters, and skin aging (Figure 7).

sec.title: Trend Topics
sec.p: In the early years (2015-2017), research was focused on technical terms such as microneedle, photography, radio waves, and RF radiation, reflecting an initial emphasis on device technology and documenting results. From 2017 to 2019, terms related to safety and adverse effects, such as erythema, edema, and devices, became more prominent.

From 2020 onward, clinical and patient-centered terms became more prominent, such as human, female, adult, middle-aged, patient satisfaction, and clinical outcome. This reflects a shift toward applied research in adult populations, emphasizing safety, patient satisfaction, and aesthetic effectiveness. In recent years (2021-2023), research has especially focused on wrinkles, RF therapy, and aesthetics, indicating a consolidation of the field around wrinkle reduction, skin rejuvenation, and patient-reported aesthetic satisfaction as key research areas (Figure 8).

sec.title: Thematic Analysis and Factorial Map
sec.p: The thematic map (Figure 9) identified 4 main clusters: motor themes (female, human, adult, and clinical outcome), representing the consolidated clinical core of the field. Basic themes, such as clinical assessment, pain assessment, and skin surface, are fundamental for the objective evaluation of treatment outcomes. Niche themes include acne, burns, and histology, reflecting specific applications such as acne scarring, burns, and histological studies. And emerging/declining themes: ultrasonic therapy, micro-focused ultrasound, endocrinology, suggesting alternative technologies under exploration or in decline.

sec.title: Factorial Map
sec.p: The factorial map clearly identifies three sections: clinical, technological, and emerging technology. A clinical section focuses on terms such as wrinkles, rejuvenation, skin, and patient satisfaction, while the technological section includes microneedles, fractional non-ablative treatments, and an electrode. An emerging technology section features micro-focused ultrasound, subsurface methods, and trials.

These findings indicate that research on RF modalities combines a consolidated clinical base with ongoing technological developments. At the same time, alternative techniques such as micro-focused ultrasound represent peripheral but expanding research frontiers (Figure 10).

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: This bibliometric analysis of RF modalities for facial and neck rejuvenation demonstrates a steady growth in scientific output between 2015 and 2025, with a peak in publications observed in 2021. This finding reflects the global rise in minimally invasive aesthetic procedures. It is consistent with previous bibliometric studies on facial rejuvenation, where a surge in academic production has paralleled the increasing clinical demand.55

Regarding geographical distribution, the United States emerged as the leading contributor both in publication volume and in accumulated citations. In contrast, South Korea and China have rapidly gained prominence over the past 5 years. However, contributions from Latin America and Africa remain scarce, with only one author identified from Colombia during the study period. This geographic gap underscores the need to promote multicenter research and international collaboration, particularly in underrepresented regions, to generate more diverse and globally applicable evidence.56

The analysis of authors and institutions revealed a concentration of productivity, with Goldman MP standing out as the most influential author and central figure within co-authorship networks.11-14 Strengthening collaborative networks could facilitate knowledge transfer, enhance the diversity of study populations, and contribute to the standardization of treatment protocols.

In terms of publication sources, Dermatologic Surgery and the Journal of Cosmetic Dermatology accounted for a significant proportion of articles, highlighting their role as specialized dissemination channels in aesthetic dermatology.

The thematic evolution of research illustrates a clear transition in the field's priorities. In the early years (2015-2017), studies mainly focused on technical parameters and device-related aspects (eg, microneedle and radio waves). During 2017-2019, research emphasis shifted toward safety and adverse effects (erythema, edema). From 2020 onward, dominant themes have been clinical and patient-centered outcomes (patient satisfaction, clinical outcomes, and esthetics), reflecting a maturation of the field toward patient experience-driven aesthetic medicine. This transition aligns with the growing consensus that patient-reported outcome measures should be key indicators of success in cosmetic procedures.57

The findings of thematic analysis suggest that fractional nonablative RF currently sits at the intersection of clinical consolidation and ongoing technological innovation. In contrast, other energy-based modalities may expand or decline depending on future evidence.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: This bibliometric analysis presents some methodological limitations. First, the exclusive use of the Scopus database may have restricted the comprehensiveness of the results, as records from other sources, such as Web of Science or Dimensions, were not included. This limitation may also constrain the analytical capacity of the applied bibliometric tools. Furthermore, the inclusion of English-language articles only may have excluded relevant scientific publications in other languages, particularly from regions with high activity in aesthetic dermatology, thereby introducing a potential language bias into the findings.

sec.title: CONCLUSIONS
sec.p: In conclusion, this bibliometric analysis demonstrates that research on nonablative RF for facial and neck rejuvenation with user-oriented metrics has experienced steady growth over the past decade. The thematic evolution of the field highlights a shift from technical and safety-focused studies toward patient-centered outcomes, particularly satisfaction and esthetic efficacy, reflecting a maturation of the discipline. However, the concentration of publications in a limited number of authors, institutions, and journals, along with the underrepresentation of regions such as Latin America and Africa, underscores the need for broader international collaboration, standardized protocols, and greater integration of patient-reported outcomes to strengthen the evidence base and guide future clinical practice.

sec.title: Supplemental Material
sec.p: This article contains supplemental material located online at https://doi.org/10.1093/asjof/ojaf149.

sec.title: Disclosures
sec.p: Dr Kumar is a consultant for Jeisys Medical Inc. (Seoul, Republic of South Korea) and receives a fee, whereas Ms Baek is an employee who receives a salary. The other authors declared no potential conflicts of interest with respect to the research, authorship, and publication of this article.
